key_departures.impact: Super Bowl LX MVP departed to the Chiefs on the richest RB free agent deal ever. Walker rushed for 1,027 yards in the regular season and went supernova in the playoffs. Zach Charbonnet is recovering from a torn ACL, leaving Seattle with only depth-level backs (Holani, McIntosh, Akers) on the healthy roster.

Starting safety who moved from slot CB and excelled as a versatile playmaker in Mike Macdonald's defense. Produced 7 INTs over last two seasons. Seattle attempted a last-minute re-sign offer but could not agree on terms. Leaves Julian Love (injury-prone) and tendered ERFA Ty Okada as the only available safeties on the 53-man roster.

Departed to Bengals on a $60M deal, largest of the three Seahawks departures. Mafe played in a deep EDGE rotation and had declining sack numbers (9 in 2023 → 6 in 2024 → 2 in 2025), but had elite pass rush win rate (8th in NFL per ESPN). His exit opens a hole in EDGE rotation behind DeMarcus Lawrence, though Seattle has shown confidence in younger options (Nwosu, Morris, etc.).

Lost starting job to Josh Jobe in September 2025 and never reclaimed it. Seahawks reportedly open to trading him at the trade deadline. With Jobe re-signed, Woolen's departure was expected and the team had already planned around it. Creates a CB3 void but Jobe handles the CB2 role. Nehemiah Pritchett is internal depth option.

Non-tendered as restricted free agent. Caught only 2 passes in the regular season but contributed on special teams and had a TD catch in the NFC Championship. Depth/special teams loss only; Seattle expected to seek a lower-cost reunion deal.
Backup OT departed as UFA. Cross and Lucas are entrenched starters; this is a depth loss only.
Special teams standout and LB depth departed as UFA. Minimal impact on depth chart given Thomas and Jones IV return.
Special teams WR departed as UFA. Minimal offensive impact.

signings.analysis: Re-signed on a 3yr/$51M deal with $34.7M guaranteed — significant multi-year commitment. Shaheed was primarily a special teams weapon in 2025 (three return TDs including two in playoffs) with limited offensive production post-trade (266 scrimmage yards over 12 games). The AAV of $17M is mid-to-upper starter tier for WR. With JSN in line for a massive extension and Kupp at $13.5M, this locks in the WR room despite light pass-catching production from Shaheed to date. WR was not a declared need pre-FA. This re-signing ensures their elite return game stays intact and gives Darnold another weapon with a full offseason together.

Re-signed to 3yr/$24M ($8M AAV). Jobe was statistically one of the best coverage CBs in the league in 2025 — 5.4 yards per target allowed (4th among 61 CBs targeted 50+ times), 47.7% completion rate (4th), 71.1 passer rating allowed (5th). He is solidified as the CB2 behind Devon Witherspoon. However, with Riq Woolen departing, Seattle still needs CB3 depth. The guarantee structure is not yet confirmed but the multi-year deal at a reasonable AAV signals real investment. CB need moves from potential crisis (losing both Woolen and Jobe) to a managed depth need. Partially addressed because Woolen's exit still leaves CB depth thin.

Re-signed on a 2yr/$8M extension as a restricted free agent, keeping both starting ILBs (Thomas and Ernest Jones IV) locked in for 2026. Thomas emerged from special teams depth to a full-time starter in 2025, posting 96 tackles, 3.5 sacks, and an INT. At $4M AAV this is tremendous value for a starting caliber LB. LB was not a critical need pre-FA; with both starters retained, it is fully resolved.

Tendered as an exclusive rights free agent — a roster-retention move, not a meaningful address of the RB need created by Walker's departure. Holani is depth-level talent, not a proven starter. With Charbonnet on ACL recovery, Holani is penciled in as the lead back by default but is unproven in that role. RB remains an urgent need.

Tendered as an exclusive rights free agent. Okada made 11 fill-in starts in 2025 due to injuries and showed promise (1 INT, 6 PBUs, 1.5 sacks, 3 TFL). He is the projected free safety starter following Bryant's departure. However, an ERFA tender is minimum cost with no market validation — this is an internal promotion, not a free agency address. Okada could succeed, but depth behind him is razor-thin (Love is injury-prone; Finley has limited action). Safety remains a need, especially for depth/insurance.

1yr/$1.1M minimum-tier deal. Bell is a special teams standout who had 14 appearances (2 starts) in 2025. Not a starter-quality player. Keeps the safety room from being completely bare behind Love and Okada but does not solve the need created by Bryant's departure. Safety need unchanged in terms of starter quality.

Brought back as Sam Darnold's backup. Lock spent 2022-2023 in Seattle and started 4 games in 2023. 1-year deal; backup-only role. QB is not a need with Darnold under contract. This is roster insurance only.
Veteran journeyman TE signed at minimum-level terms. Pass-catching depth behind Elijah Arroyo and A.J. Barner. No meaningful impact on any need — TE depth move only.

Depth receiver and return specialist candidate signed on a minimum-type deal. Addresses the WR/returner depth thinned by Shaheed's potential departure from the return role if she takes a larger offensive role. Primarily a camp competition and special teams body.
Veteran depth WR signed at minimum. Fourth or fifth receiver at best. No impact on declared needs.
Re-signed LB depth and special teams contributor. With Thomas and Jones IV locked in as starters, Ross is rotation/ST depth. No impact on needs picture.

recommended_need_adjustments.reasoning: Kenneth Walker III (Super Bowl MVP, 1,027 rush yards) signed with Kansas City on a 3yr/$45M record deal. Zach Charbonnet is recovering from a torn ACL. The only healthy backfield options are ERFA-tendered George Holani, Kenny McIntosh, Cam Akers, Velus Jones Jr., and Jacardia Wright — none of whom are proven three-down NFL starters. This is now the most urgent need on the roster and the clearest draft priority. Tier 1 — must address in Round 1-3 of draft or via free agency addition.

Coby Bryant departed to Chicago on a 3yr/$40M deal ($25.75M gtd). ERFA Ty Okada is projected starter at FS after 11 fill-in starts; Julian Love starts at SS but missed 9 games in 2025 due to injury. D'Anthony Bell provides minimum-level depth only. The safety room is thin and carries significant injury risk. Seahawks met with safety prospects at the Combine (Theinemen, Wheatley, Ramsey). Tier 2 — should target in Rounds 1-3 of the draft, could add a mid-tier veteran FA.

Boye Mafe departed to Bengals on a 3yr/$60M deal. However, DeMarcus Lawrence (3yr/$42M, 2025 signing) anchors the group, and Seattle has internal depth in Uchenna Nwosu and younger options (Morris, Ivey, etc.). Mafe's declining production (2 sacks in 2025) means this is more of a rotation loss than a starter loss. EDGE remains a need for depth/rotation development but is not a crisis. Tier 3 — mid-round draft target or veteran minimum depth signing.

Josh Jobe re-signed on 3yr/$24M, solidifying the CB2 role behind Devon Witherspoon. Riq Woolen departed (lost starting job in Week 10, expected exit). Nehemiah Pritchett (2024 5th-rounder) is depth but unproven as a starter. CB3/depth is the residual need — the team is stable at CB1 (Witherspoon) and CB2 (Jobe) but lacks a proven third corner. Tier 3 — Day 2-3 draft pick or low-cost veteran. This is a deep CB draft class.

Shaheed re-signed on 3yr/$51M ($34.7M gtd). JSN is the WR1 with a massive extension coming. Cooper Kupp returns at $13.5M. Shaheed is WR3/returner. The room has depth with Tory Horton as WR4. WR need is resolved at the starter level — only need is late-round developmental depth. Tier 4 — luxury pick only.

Both starting ILBs return — Ernest Jones IV (re-signed last offseason) and Drake Thomas (2yr/$8M extension). With both starters locked in and Josh Ross re-signed for depth, LB is fully resolved. Not a draft need.

No movement at OT. Charles Cross and Abe Lucas are entrenched starters. Josh Jones departed but was depth only. Seahawks have younger interior/tackle developmental options. Still a position to watch for depth in the draft but tier unchanged.

key_additions.headline: Playoff hero and elite returner kept on three-year deal; three return TDs (including two in playoffs) made him pivotal to Super Bowl run
Top-5 coverage CB in 2025 by yards-per-target and completion rate re-signs as CB2 behind Witherspoon at below-market AAV
Breakout 2025 starter (96 tackles, 3.5 sacks) locked in on team-friendly extension, keeping both starting LBs intact
Familiar backup returns to back up Sam Darnold; started 4 games for SEA in 2023
Special teams ace returns at minimum cost to provide depth behind Okada and Love post-Bryant departure

key_losses.headline: Super Bowl LX MVP and 1,027-yard rusher departs on NFL's richest RB free agent deal ever, leaving a glaring backfield void with Charbonnet on ACL recovery
Versatile starting safety who totaled 7 INTs over two seasons departs, thinning a secondary now reliant on ERFA Ty Okada and injury-prone Julian Love
Former 2nd-round pick cashes in with Cincinnati despite declining sack totals; 8th-best pass rush win rate in 2025 disguised his undervalued contribution to Seattle's top-ranked defense
Former Pro Bowl CB and 12-INT career Seahawk departs after losing starting job in 2025; expected exit with Jobe signed as his replacement

notes: All contract figures sourced from ESPN (Schefter/Fowler), NFL Network (Pelissero/Rapoport), Spotrac, and OverTheCap. Josh Jobe guarantee structure not yet officially confirmed — reported as 3yr/$24M. Drake Thomas guarantee structure per OTC reported as low given RFA extension structure. D'Anthony Bell signed in 2025 FA cycle (March 2025) and re-appears as a returning player — his 2026 contract status is UFA after the 2025 deal expired; the seahawks.com primer references him as a new 2026 signing but Spotrac/OTC still show his 2025 deal. This report treats him as a re-signing for 2026 based on official team news. George Holani and Ty Okada are ERFA tenders — contract values are league-minimum level and not yet posted as full contracts. Riq Woolen has not yet signed with a new team as of March 10, 2026 — listed as TBD/unsigned. The $42,966,262 post-FA cap space figure is sourced from OverTheCap.com as of March 10, 2026 and does not yet account for pending rookie class allocations.
